objective: Track every feature, user story, expected behavior, evidence, and current status in one canonical spreadsheet.
currentLoop: Feature inventory is complete. All identified UX/logistical regressions have been fixed and retested; remaining open items are external Wayback dependency risks.
stories: As a researcher, I can submit one URL to `save` and receive a structured archived snapshot result.
Returns JSON with `mode=save`, `archived_url`, `timestamp`, and `source` after direct save or fallback.
UOL article saved on 2026-07-02 with timestamp 20260702061936 via availability_api.
As a user who only needs a stable archived URL, I can accept an existing snapshot returned by save fallback.
If no fresh save redirect is seen but a usable snapshot exists, `save` still returns success JSON.
Wikipedia article returned older snapshot 20260515135826 and was treated as successful archived URL.
As a user, I get a JSON error instead of a traceback when no snapshot can be obtained.
Returns JSON `{ error: ... }` with non-zero exit when save cannot obtain any snapshot.
x.com SamPancher status URL returned `Unable to obtain an archived snapshot...` as JSON.
As a user, I can ask whether a URL is already archived without creating a new save.
Returns `available: true/false` plus `snapshot` object or null.
UOL URL returned available=true; x.com URL returned available=false.
Check nearest available snapshot by timestamp
As a user, I can query availability close to a requested time.
Passes timestamp through availability API and returns closest snapshot if one is available.
As a user, I can submit multiple URLs positionally in one batch run.
Returns `mode=batch-save`, `total`, and one result/error object per URL.
Batch run over example.com + UOL produced one success and one per-URL timeout error without crashing.
Network variability can make one URL fail while others succeed.
As a user, I can load URLs from a file instead of only positional args.
Reads file lines and appends them to the batch URL list.
No direct unit test for CLI path
Manual batch run with `--input tests/fixtures/wayback_archive/urls_with_comments.txt` returned one processed result entry sourced from the file.
As a user, duplicate URLs from args or file are only processed once.
Deduplicates combined URL list before processing.
Manual batch run with duplicate example.com entries in `tests/fixtures/wayback_archive/urls_with_comments.txt` returned `total: 1`.
As a user, one failing URL does not abort the entire batch.
Each URL is wrapped independently and errors are stored beside successes.
Batch run preserved success for example.com and error for UOL timeout in same result set.
As a user, I get a clear error if I invoke batch-save with no URLs and no input file.
Returns JSON error `batch-save requires at least one URL or --input`.
Manual run with no args returned the expected JSON error.
As a user, I can keep comments and blank lines in my URL file.
Lines starting with `#` and empty lines are skipped.
Manual batch run against `tests/fixtures/wayback_archive/urls_with_comments.txt` processed only one URL, proving comments and blank lines were ignored.
As a user, I can list archived snapshots for one URL.
Returns `mode=history`, `count`, and `snapshots[]` with CDX-derived fields plus `archived_url`.
Manual `history http://tc.eserver.org/ --limit 5` returned 5 snapshot rows with digest/status/original/archived_url fields.
As a user, I can limit or time-bound history requests.
Builds CDX URL with `limit`, `from`, and `to` query params.
No direct unit test of query combinations
Manual `history https://tc.eserver.org/ --from-timestamp 20181115172501 --to-timestamp 20181115172501` returned the exact 20181115172501 snapshot.
Verified manually, but still depends on CDX responsiveness.
As a user, transient CDX 5xx or timeout failures are retried automatically.
Retries up to 3 attempts with 2s, then 4s backoff before failing.
Observed in practice that compare/history sometimes recover after retries on tc.eserver.
Still external dependency; retries reduce but do not eliminate failures.
As a user, I get a clear CDX unavailable error after retries are exhausted.
Returns JSON error naming CDX endpoint unavailability after N attempts.
Manual `history http://tc.eserver.org/` returned `CDX endpoint unavailable after 3 attempt(s): HTTP Error 503`.
As a user, I can request the snapshot closest to a specific timestamp.
Returns `mode=nearest` and a `snapshot` with `requested_timestamp`, `timestamp`, `source`, and archived URL.
UOL URL with timestamp 20260702061900 returned 20260702061936 via availability_api.
As a user, `at` behaves the same as `nearest`.
CLI alias emits the same nearest snapshot shape.
Manual `at` run on UOL returned same nearest snapshot JSON.
As a user, if availability lookup cannot resolve a nearest snapshot, the wrapper falls back to CDX-based lookup.
When availability misses, nearest first tries an exact CDX timestamp lookup and then falls back to broader CDX history, labeling `source=cdx_api`.
tc.eserver explicit timestamp lookups now resolve via exact CDX rows even when availability is false or collapsed.
As a user, I can ask the wrapper to auto-select two archived versions of the same URL for comparison.
Returns `comparison_mode=latest_different_digest`, `changes_url`, `history_count`, and two snapshot objects when CDX history is responsive.
tc.eserver auto-compare eventually returned `history_count=683` and distinct left/right snapshots.
Auto mode still depends on CDX responsiveness.
As a user, if auto-compare cannot read history, I am told what to do next.
Returns error suggesting retry later or rerun compare with explicit timestamps.
Manual tc.eserver auto compare with `--timeout 10` returned actionable guidance mentioning `--from-timestamp` and `--to-timestamp`.
As a user, I can compare the snapshots nearest two explicit requested times.
tc.eserver explicit compare returned left 20180427130634 and right 20181115172501 after probing scheme variants and exact CDX rows.
As a user, if availability collapses both requested times to the same snapshot, the wrapper retries both sides via CDX before accepting the result.
When timestamps differ but snapshots collide, wrapper re-resolves via CDX and only returns success if left/right are truly distinct.
tc.eserver explicit compare originally collapsed; after fix it re-resolved to distinct 20180427130634 and 20181115172501 snapshots.
As a user, I do not get a misleading compare result if both requested times still map to the same snapshot.
Raises a clear error telling the user the two requested timestamps resolved to the same snapshot.
Covered by automated test that forces both timestamps to remain collapsed after CDX retry.
As a user, if availability collapses and CDX fallback also fails, I get a targeted error.
Raises error explaining that availability collapsed both timestamps and CDX could not disambiguate them.
Covered by automated test that forces CDX disambiguation failure after collapsed availability results.
As a user, compare results include a direct Wayback changes UI URL.
Seen in tc.eserver auto and explicit compare outputs.
As a user, save succeeds even if the archive response is surfaced through an HTTP error with `Location`.
Treats HTTP error with Wayback Location as success and extracts timestamp.
Covered by automated test that simulates an HTTP error carrying a Wayback `Location` header.
As a user, save succeeds when the archive endpoint returns a final redirect URL directly.
Direct redirected archive URL is treated as success.
Covered by automated test that simulates a direct save redirect response.
As a user, each command returns structured JSON rather than prose.
Success responses include `mode`; failures include `{ error: ... }` via top-level exception handler.
Covered indirectly across all unit tests
Observed manually across save/available/batch-save/nearest/compare/history failure outputs, including newly validated input errors.
As a user, malformed timestamp values are rejected locally with a clear JSON error instead of causing a traceback or ambiguous network behavior.
Commands that accept timestamp flags return `{ error: ... }` when given a non-14-digit or impossible calendar timestamp.
Manual `nearest https://example.com --timestamp nope`, `available https://example.com --timestamp nope`, and `history https://example.com --from-timestamp nope` each returned structured JSON errors.
As a user, I cannot accidentally request explicit compare mode with only one timestamp argument.
Returns JSON error requiring both `--from-timestamp` and `--to-timestamp` together, or neither.
Manual `compare https://example.com --from-timestamp 20260101120000` returned the expected structured JSON error.
As a user, a missing batch input file returns a structured JSON error instead of a Python traceback.
Returns `{ error: ... }` when the input file cannot be read.
Manual `batch-save --input /Users/breno/Documents/code/PROJECTS/HRF_GRANT/freedom-skills/tmp/wayback_archive_comment_file.txt` returned a structured JSON error after the UX fix.
issues: CDX endpoint intermittently returns 503, timeout, or connection reset errors.
Retries + actionable JSON errors implemented; still depends on Wayback CDX availability.
Manual history/compare auto runs on tc.eserver and Wikipedia; resolved only partially because dependency is external.
Batch-save can mix successful and timed-out URLs in the same run because each URL has independent network behavior.
Current behavior preserves per-URL result/error instead of aborting batch.
Manual batch run: example.com succeeded while UOL timed out.
Skill docs originally implied auto compare was reliable without timestamps.
SKILL.md now marks auto compare as best-effort and recommends explicit timestamps.
Fixed in current branch during this audit.
Explicit compare could return the same snapshot for two different requested timestamps when availability collapsed both sides or returned nothing for one scheme.
Wrapper now probes `http`/`https`, retries both sides via exact CDX lookup, and errors if the result still collapses.
Reproduced and fixed using tc.eserver explicit compare story.
CDX failures previously surfaced as raw transport failures or tracebacks, especially after availability misses.
Wrapper now retries and returns clearer guidance on next steps; explicit compare avoids unnecessary full-history fetches when an exact CDX row exists.
Fixed in current branch during this audit.
Malformed timestamps, missing batch input files, and partial explicit compare arguments previously produced tracebacks or silently triggered the wrong mode.
The wrapper now validates timestamp syntax, requires compare timestamps as a pair, and converts unreadable input files into structured JSON errors.
Reproduced manually, fixed in code, and retested through CLI and unit coverage.
